sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
All 3 actions succeeded! The player went from (9, 9) to (7, 9) to (7, 7) to (7, 5). The LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) did NOT cause a game over, even though it's near the B cell at (6, 6).
So the pattern is more nuanced. The LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) has the B cell at (6, 6) 1 row above, but it didn't cause a game over. The LEFT from (5, 7) to (5, 5) has the B cell at (4, 6) 1 row above, and it DID cause a game over.
The difference might be the row. Row 5 is closer to the red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5), while row 7 is further away. Or maybe the B cell at (4, 6) is "more dangerous" than the B cell at (6, 6).
Looking at the image, the player (blue dot) is at (7, 5). From here, I can go:
- UP to (5, 5): intermediate (6, 5) = g, dest (5, 5) = W. Valid.
- DOWN to (9, 5): (9, 5) doesn't exist. Invalid.
- LEFT to (7, 3): (7, 3) doesn't exist. Invalid.
- RIGHT to (7, 7): back. Valid but useless.
So from (7, 5), the only useful move is UP to (5, 5).
From (5, 5), I can go:
- LEFT to (5, 3): intermediate (5, 4) = g, dest (5, 3) = W. Valid. But this might cause a game over (B cell at (4, 6) is 1 row above).
- RIGHT to (5, 7): back. Valid but useless.
- UP to (3, 5): (3, 5) = R (red trap). Valid but might cause game over.
- DOWN to (7, 5): back. Valid but useless.
Hmm, from (5, 5), the LEFT to (5, 3) might cause a game over. Let me think about this.
The LEFT from (5, 7) to (5, 5) caused a game over because the B cell at (4, 6) is 1 row above. The LEFT from (5, 5) to (5, 3) would have the B cell at (4, 6) 1 row above and 3 columns to the right. The red traps at (3, 3) and (3, 5) are 2 rows above. So the animation bounding box might include the red traps and the B cell, causing a game over.
But wait, the LEFT from (7, 7) to (7, 5) didn't cause a game over, even though the B cell at (6, 6) is 1 row above. So maybe the row matters. Row 5 is "dangerous" for horizontal moves, but row 7 is not.
Let me try UP from (7, 5) to (5, 5) and see what happens. If it succeeds, I'll try LEFT from (5, 5) to (5, 3) and see if it causes a game over.
Actually, let me think about the path from (7, 5) to the target (9, 3). From (7, 5), I can go:
- UP to (5, 5): valid
- From (5, 5), LEFT to (5, 3): might cause game over
- From (5, 3), LEFT to (5, 1): might cause game over
- From (5, 1), DOWN to (7, 1): valid
- From (7, 1), DOWN to (9, 1): valid
- From (9, 1), RIGHT to (9, 3): valid
But the LEFT moves on row 5 might cause game overs. Let me try a different approach.
From (7, 5), I can go:
- UP to (5, 5): valid
- From (5, 5), UP to (3, 5): (3, 5) = R (red trap). Valid but might cause game over.
- From (3, 5), LEFT to (3, 3): (3, 3) = R (red trap). Valid but might cause game over.
- From (3, 3), DOWN to (5, 3): intermediate (4, 3) doesn't exist. Invalid.
Hmm, this doesn't work.
Let me try another approach. From (7, 5), I can go:
- UP to (5, 5): valid
- From (5, 5), LEFT to (5, 3): might cause game over
Let me just try it and see. If it causes a game over, I'll know that horizontal moves on row 5 are dangerous.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The player is at (7, 5). The target is at (9, 3). The player needs to get to (9, 1) and then RIGHT to (9, 3).
From (7, 5), the player can go UP to (5, 5), then LEFT to (5, 3), then LEFT to (5, 1), then DOWN to (7, 1), then DOWN to (9, 1), then RIGHT to (9, 3).
But the LEFT moves on row 5 might cause game overs. Let me try UP, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT from (7, 5).
Actually, let me first try UP from (7, 5) to (5, 5) and see if it succeeds. Then I'll try LEFT from (5, 5) to (5, 3) and see if it causes a game over.
